title: Air-fryer meatballs
subtitle: Evenly browned beef meatballs without frying
excerpt: Beef meatballs seasoned with garlic and parsley, shaped and cooked in the air fryer at 200°C for 12 minutes. The air fryer browns all sides evenly as the circulating heat reaches every angle of the ball, avoiding the flat patch that a frying pan always leaves on the base.
sourceNotes: Meatballs appear across European and Middle Eastern cooking under names including polpette (Italian) and köfte (Turkish). The British home version most often appears as Italian-style meatballs served with pasta and tomato sauce. The air fryer produces a more consistently browned meatball than a frying pan because the heat circulates around the ball rather than only contacting the base.

content.text: Keep the mixture cold. Warm mince fat softens and the meatballs lose their shape and become greasy during cooking. Mix quickly, shape quickly, then chill for at least 20 minutes before the air fryer. Cold fat holds the ball together as the exterior sets.

Combine the {{breadcrumbs-panko}} with the {{whole-milk}} and leave to soak for 2 minutes. Add the {{beef-mince}}, soaked breadcrumbs, {{eggs}}, {{garlic}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}} to a bowl. Mix until just combined: overworking makes the meatballs dense and tight. Divide into 20 equal portions (about 30g each) and roll each into a ball. Refrigerate for 20 minutes. Preheat the air fryer to 200°C for 2 minutes. Place the meatballs in the basket in a single layer, not touching. Cook at 200°C for 12 minutes, shaking the basket once at the 6-minute mark, until browned all over and cooked through.

items.fix: Chill for longer before cooking. If the mixture is very wet, add an extra tablespoon of breadcrumbs. Handle cold: warm hands melt the fat and break down the structure
items.cause: Mixture too wet, not chilled, or too little binder
items.symptom: Meatballs falling apart in the basket

items.fix: Mix until just combined, no more. Very lean mince (under 10% fat) produces dry, dense meatballs. Use mince with 15 to 20% fat content
items.cause: Mixture overworked, or mince too lean

items.fix: Keep meatballs at about 30g each. Overcrowding steams rather than browns them. Cook in two batches if the basket is full
items.cause: Meatballs too large, or basket overcrowded
items.symptom: Exterior browned but centre still pink after 12 minutes

content.text: Air-fryer meatballs go directly into a tomato sauce and serve with pasta, sit in a sub roll with melted mozzarella, or work as a quick weeknight protein alongside rice and greens. The air fryer saves the pan-frying step and produces a more consistently round meatball than any stovetop method.
